Naboth may have been pleased to have a vineyard so near the palace, but this situation proved fatal to him; many a person's possessions have become their snare, and being close to greatness has had bad consequences. Discontent is a sin that punishes itself, and makes people torment themselves. It is a sin that is its own parent; it arises not from circumstances, but from the mind: we find Paul contented in prison, while Ahab was discontented in a palace. He had all the delights of Canaan, that pleasant land, at his disposal; the wealth of a kingdom, the pleasures of court, and the honors and powers of a throne; yet all is nothing to him without Naboth's vineyard. Wrong desires expose people to continual annoyances, and those who are inclined to fret, however well off, may always find something to fret about.

When a man has an agent for Satan instead of a suitable helper, in the form of an artful, unprincipled, yet beloved wife, fatal effects may be expected. Never were more wicked orders given by any prince than those Jezebel sent to the rulers of Jezreel. Naboth must be murdered under the guise of religion. There is no wickedness so vile, so horrid, but religion has sometimes been made a cover for it. Also, it must be done under the guise of justice, and with the formalities of legal process. Let us, from this sad story, be amazed at the wickedness of the wicked, and the power of Satan in the children of disobedience. Let us commit the keeping of our lives and comforts to God, for innocence will not always be our security; and let us rejoice in the knowledge that all will be set right on the great day.

Blessed Paul complains that he was sold under sin, as a poor captive against his will; but Ahab was willing; he sold himself to sin, of choice, and as his own act. Jezebel, his wife, stirred him up to do wickedly. Ahab is reproved, and his sin set before his eyes, by Elijah. That person's condition is very miserable who has made the word of God his enemy; and very desperate who counts the ministers of that word his enemies because they tell him the truth. Ahab put on the appearance of a penitent, yet his heart was unhumbled and unchanged. Ahab’s repentance was only what could be seen by people; it was outward only. Let this encourage all who truly repent and sincerely believe the holy gospel, that if a pretending, partial penitent shall go to his house relieved, surely, a sincere, believing penitent shall go to his house justified.
